import pygame import random import requests import base64 from io import BytesIO # Define the screen dimensions SCREEN_WIDTH = 1500 SCREEN_HEIGHT = 800 # Define the colors BLACK = (0, 0, 0) # Define the artists and their monthly Spotify listeners artists = { "$NOT": {"listeners": 7781046}, "21 Savage": {"listeners": 60358167}, "9lokknine": {"listeners": 1680245}, "A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie": {"listeners": 18379137}, } # Initialize the game pygame.init() # Define the font and font size for the artist names font = pygame.font.Font(None, 36) listeners_font = pygame.font.Font(None, 24) question_font = pygame.font.Font(None, 36) # Initialize the game window and caption screen = pygame.display.set_mode((SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT)) pygame.display.set_caption("Guess the Artist") # Update the display once pygame.display.update() # Create a clock object to control the frame rate clock = pygame.time.Clock() score = 0 # Function to fetch the artist image using the Spotify API def fetch_artist_image(artist, client_id, client_secret): # Base64 encode the client ID and secret client_credentials = base64.b64encode(f"{client_id}:{client_secret}".encode()).decode() # Request an access token token_url = "https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token" headers = { "Authorization": f"Basic {client_credentials}" } data = { "grant_type": "client_credentials" } response = requests.post(token_url, headers=headers, data=data) access_token = response.json()["access_token"] # Search for the artist on Spotify search_url = f"https://api.spotify.com/v1/search?q={artist}&type=artist" headers = { "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}" } response = requests.get(search_url, headers=headers) data = response.json() # Get the first artist result artist_info = data["artists"]["items"][0] # Get the image URL of the artist image_url = artist_info["images"][0]["url"] # Download the image and return its contents image_data = requests.get(image_url).content return image_data # Function to check the user's guess def check_guess(artist1, artist2, guess): if guess == "1" and artists[artist1]["listeners"] > artists[artist2]["listeners"]: return True elif guess == "2" and artists[artist2]["listeners"] > artists[artist1]["listeners"]: return True else: return False # Game loop running = True while running: for event in pygame.event.get(): if event.type == pygame.QUIT: running = False # Game logic correct_guess = False while not correct_guess: # Clear the screen screen.fill(BLACK) # Select two random artists first_artist, second_artist = random.sample(list(artists.keys()), 2) # Fetch the artist images client_id = '048322b8ea61411a826de68d0cb1c6d2' client_secret = '29f94d572c6240cf99fc3f13ce050503' first_artist_image_data = fetch_artist_image(first_artist, client_id, client_secret) second_artist_image_data = fetch_artist_image(second_artist, client_id, client_secret) # Load the image data using Pygame first_artist_image = pygame.image.load(BytesIO(first_artist_image_data)).convert() second_artist_image = pygame.image.load(BytesIO(second_artist_image_data)).convert() # Render the text surfaces for the artist names first_artist_text = font.render(f"1 - {first_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255)) second_artist_text = font.render(f"2 - {second_artist.title()}", True, (255, 255, 255)) # Render the question text question_text = question_font.render("Which artist has more listeners -", True, (255, 255, 255)) # Render the text surfaces for the listeners' count first_listeners_text = listeners_font.render(f"{artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} listeners", True, (255, 255, 255)) second_listeners_text = listeners_font.render(f"{artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} listeners", True, (255, 255, 255)) # Display the artist images with a gap for the question image_y = 100 + question_text.get_height() + 40 screen.blit(first_artist_image, (0, image_y)) screen.blit(second_artist_image, (SCREEN_WIDTH // 2, image_y)) # Calculate the center position for the artist names first_x = (first_artist_image.get_width() - first_artist_text.get_width()) // 2 second_x = SCREEN_WIDTH // 2 + (second_artist_image.get_width() - second_artist_text.get_width()) // 2 # question on pygame position question_x = (SCREEN_WIDTH - question_text.get_width()) // 2 question_y = 50 # Display the artist names centered under the image screen.blit(first_artist_text, (first_x, first_artist_image.get_height() + 10)) screen.blit(second_artist_text, (second_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 10)) # Display the question text screen.blit(question_text, (question_x, question_y)) pygame.display.flip() # Prompt the player for their guess guess = input(f"
Which artist has more monthly Spotify listeners - 1. {first_artist.title()} or 2. {second_artist.title()}? ") guess_lower = guess.strip().lower() # Calculate the center position for the listeners' count first_listeners_x = (first_artist_image.get_width() - first_listeners_text.get_width()) // 2 second_listeners_x = SCREEN_WIDTH // 2 + (second_artist_image.get_width() - second_listeners_text.get_width()) // 2 # Display the listeners' count centered under the image screen.blit(first_listeners_text, (first_listeners_x, first_artist_image.get_height() + 40)) screen.blit(second_listeners_text, (second_listeners_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 40)) pygame.display.flip() if guess_lower == 'quit': print("Thanks for playing!") running = False break elif guess_lower not in ['1', '2', first_artist.lower(), second_artist.lower()]: print("Invalid input. Please enter the name/number of one of the two artists or 'quit' to end the game.") elif check_guess(first_artist, second_artist, guess_lower): print(f"You guessed correctly! {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.") correct_guess = True score += 1 else: print(f"
Sorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.
") print(f"Your current score is {score}.") # Ask the player if they want to play again play_again = input("Would you like to play again? (y/n) ") if play_again.lower() == 'n': print(f"Your final score is {score}. Thanks for playing!") running = False clock.tick(60) # Quit the game pygame.quit() why doesnt the score reset after making a mistake

The score does not reset after making a mistake in the provided code because the code does not include a line to reset the score back to 0. 

To reset the score after making a mistake, you can add `score = 0` after the line `running = True`. This will set the score back to 0 every time the game loop starts again, allowing the player to start with a fresh score each game. 

Here is the updated code:

```python
# Game loop
running = True
while running:
    score = 0  # Reset the score to 0
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
            running = False

    # Game logic
    correct_guess = False
    while not correct_guess:
        # Clear the screen
        screen.fill(BLACK)

        # Rest of the code...
```

With this update, the score will reset to 0 each time the game loop starts, allowing the player to accumulate a new score in each game.

# Create a clock object to control the frame rate
clock = pygame.time.Clock()

score = 0
then why is this in the code

Apologies for the confusion. The line `score = 0` in the provided code is meant to initialize the score variable at the beginning of the game loop. This line ensures that the score starts at 0 every time the game loop starts, allowing the player to accumulate points for each correct guess.

However, if you want the score to reset to 0 after making a mistake, you can add `score = 0` immediately after printing the incorrect guess message. This will reset the score to 0 every time the player makes a mistake.

Here is an example of where you can add the `score = 0` line to reset the score after making a mistake:

```python
else:
    print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
    score = 0  # Reset the score to 0

print(f"Your current score is {score}.")
```

By adding this line, the score will reset to 0 after every incorrect guess, allowing the player to start again from 0.
how do i change the positiotn of the artists names and listeners count

To change the position of the artists' names and listeners' counts in the provided code, you can modify the values passed to the `screen.blit()` function that displays the text surfaces.

Currently, the positions of the artists' names and listeners' counts are determined by the variables `first_x`, `second_x`, `first_listeners_x`, and `second_listeners_x` calculated based on the width of the artist images.

To change the positions, you can update the values of these variables according to your desired coordinates.

For example, if you want to move the artists' names to the top left corner of the screen, you can set `first_x` and `second_x` to a specific value, such as 10, and `first_listeners_x` and `second_listeners_x` to a value that aligns the listeners' counts with the artists' names.

Here is an example of how you can update the positions of the artists' names and listeners' counts:

```python
# Calculate the center position for the artist names
first_x = 10
second_x = SCREEN_WIDTH // 2 + 10

# Calculate the position for the listeners' count
first_listeners_x = 10
second_listeners_x = SCREEN_WIDTH // 2 + 10

# Display the artist names centered under the image
screen.blit(first_artist_text, (first_x, 10))
screen.blit(second_artist_text, (second_x, 10))

# Display the listeners' count centered under the image
screen.blit(first_listeners_text, (first_listeners_x, first_artist_image.get_height() + 40))
screen.blit(second_listeners_text, (second_listeners_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 40))
```

With these updates, the artists' names and listeners' counts will be positioned according to the new values of `first_x`, `second_x`, `first_listeners_x`, and `second_listeners_x`, allowing you to customize their positions within the game window.

Apologies for the confusion. The updated code snippet provided in the previous response is indeed incorrect.

To change the position of the artists' names and listeners' counts in the game window, you need to modify the `x` and `y` coordinates passed to the `screen.blit()` function. The `x` coordinate determines the horizontal position, and the `y` coordinate determines the vertical position.

Here's an example of how you can update the positions of the artists' names and listeners' counts:

```python
# Update the positions of the artist names
first_x = 200
second_x = SCREEN_WIDTH // 2 + 200

screen.blit(second_artist_text, (second_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 10))

# Display the listeners' counts

screen.blit(second_listeners_text, (second_listeners_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 40))
```

In this example, the `first_x`, `second_x`, `first_listeners_x`, and `second_listeners_x` variables are updated to set the desired horizontal positions of the text surfaces.

Please adjust the values of the `x` coordinates according to your desired positioning. Additionally, you can modify the `y` coordinates to adjust the vertical positioning if needed.

how can i make the game stop for a few seconds after making a guess so tha the listener amount is readable for the player

To make the game stop for a few seconds after the player makes a guess, you can utilize the `pygame.time.wait()` function to pause the game loop execution for a specified duration.

Here's an example of how you can modify the code to add a pause of 2 seconds (2000 milliseconds) after the player makes a guess:

```python
import pygame
import random
import requests
import base64
from io import BytesIO

# Rest of the code...

# Pause duration in milliseconds
pause_duration = 2000

# Game loop
running = True
while running:

    # Rest of the code...

    # Display the listeners' count centered under the image
    screen.blit(first_listeners_text, (first_listeners_x, first_artist_image.get_height() + 40))
    screen.blit(second_listeners_text, (second_listeners_x, second_artist_image.get_height() + 40))
    pygame.display.flip()

    if guess_lower == 'quit':
        print("Thanks for playing!")
        running = False
        break
    
    elif guess_lower not in ['1', '2', first_artist.lower(), second_artist.lower()]:
        print("Invalid input. Please enter the name/number of one of the two artists or 'quit' to end the game.")
    
    elif check_guess(first_artist, second_artist, guess_lower):
        print(f"You guessed correctly! {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.")
        correct_guess = True
        score += 1
    
    else:
        print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
        print(f"Your current score is {score}.")
        pygame.time.wait(pause_duration)  # Pause execution for 2 seconds
    
    # Ask the player if they want to play again
    play_again = input("Would you like to play again? (y/n) ")
    
    # Rest of the code...
```

With this modification, after the player makes a guess, the game will pause for 2 seconds before proceeding further. This will give the player a sufficient amount of time to read the listeners' counts before moving on to the next round. You can adjust the value of `pause_duration` to change the length of the pause according to your preferences.
the pygame window doesnt respond until the player makes a correct guess

Apologies for the oversight. The `pygame.display.flip()` function needs to be called before the `pygame.time.wait()` function in order to update the display and make the pause visible to the player.

To fix the issue, you can move the `pygame.display.flip()` function before the `pygame.time.wait()` function in the code. Here's the modified code snippet:

```python
else:
    print(f"\nSorry, you guessed incorrectly. {first_artist.title()} had {artists[first_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners and {second_artist.title()} has {artists[second_artist]['listeners']:,} monthly Spotify listeners.\n")
    print(f"Your current score is {score}.")
    pygame.display.flip()  # Update the display
    pygame.time.wait(pause_duration)  # Pause execution for 2 seconds
```

By moving `pygame.display.flip()` before `pygame.time.wait()`, the game will update the display immediately after printing the incorrect guess and show the current score to the player. Then, it will pause for the specified duration before proceeding.

This modification will make the pygame window respond and show the player's current score before waiting for a few seconds, allowing the listener amounts to be readable.
is there a  pygame.display.flip()  # Update the display function elsewhere that i need to remove

No, there doesn't seem to be a redundant `pygame.display.flip()` function call elsewhere in the provided code that needs to be removed. The `pygame.display.flip()` function is used to update the contents of the entire display surface with the current set of pixel data.

In the modified code where `pygame.display.flip()` is added before `pygame.time.wait()`, it's ensuring that the display is updated and the game window shows the current score before pausing for the specified duration.

The `pygame.display.flip()` function is typically used at the end of the loop to update the screen after all changes have been made. It is not necessary to remove any other `pygame.display.flip()` calls from the code, as it is necessary for updating the display throughout the game loop.

Therefore, you can keep the additional `pygame.display.flip()` right before `pygame.time.wait()` to ensure that the display is updated before the pause.
